causes: Wild-type GIST subtype lacking KIT/PDGFRA mutations, caused by loss of succinate dehydrogenase function; often arises with germline SDHx mutations and occurs in Carney-Stratakis syndrome and (non-germline) Carney triad, making it a hereditary/syndromic tumor.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 21173220

name: NF1-associated gastrointestinal stromal tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: GIST arising in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 due to germline NF1 loss; the inherited NF1 syndrome is the established cause, and these tumors are typically multiple and located in the small intestine.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 16143560

name: BRAF-mutant gastrointestinal stromal tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: A KIT/PDGFRA wild-type GIST driven by a somatic BRAF V600E mutation; sporadic with no established external or hereditary cause, and the mutation is a driver rather than an etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 20601955

name: Quadruple wild-type gastrointestinal stromal tumor
source: Pantaleo Cancer Med 2015 (PMID 25470732)
causes: GIST lacking KIT, PDGFRA, RAS-pathway, and SDH alterations, a heterogeneous molecular group; sporadic with no established external cause or defined predisposing condition, so the etiology is not established.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 28196638

name: Signet ring cell carcinoma of the colon and rectum
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: A poorly differentiated colorectal adenocarcinoma variant; shares colorectal cancer risk factors (chronic inflammatory bowel disease, and it is enriched in Lynch syndrome and microsatellite-unstable tumors), though many cases are sporadic with the general colorectal risk profile.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 28656894

name: Medullary carcinoma of the colon
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Characteristically a microsatellite instability high tumor caused by mismatch repair deficiency, most often sporadic MLH1 promoter hypermethylation (frequently with BRAF V600E), and a minority due to inherited Lynch syndrome; the MMR-deficient hypermutated pathway is the established basis.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 28281569

name: Colorectal neuroendocrine tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of colon or rectum; usually sporadic and often found incidentally, with no established external cause or consistent hereditary predisposition, so the etiology is not established.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; NCI

name: Colorectal mucinous adenocarcinoma
aliases: Mucinous adenocarcinoma of the colon and rectum
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: A colorectal adenocarcinoma subtype with abundant extracellular mucin; shares standard colorectal cancer risk factors and is enriched for microsatellite instability, so it is associated with mismatch repair deficiency and Lynch syndrome in addition to the general risk profile (inflammatory bowel disease, precursor adenomas).
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 22529085

name: Squamous cell carcinoma of the stomach
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Extremely rare primary gastric squamous cell carcinoma; no established cause specific to this entity, and it is not linked to the usual gastric adenocarcinoma drivers, so the etiology is not established.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 25320527

name: Undifferentiated carcinoma of the pancreas with osteoclast-like giant cells
aliases: Osteoclastic giant cell tumor of the pancreas
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Rare pancreatic carcinoma variant harboring KRAS mutations similar to conventional ductal adenocarcinoma; considered part of the pancreatic ductal carcinoma spectrum but with no established distinct etiologic cause, so the etiology is not established.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 24121170

name: Duodenal gangliocytic paraganglioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Rare tumor of the periampullary duodenum of uncertain histogenesis; overwhelmingly sporadic with no established cause, though a minority occur in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1, and for most cases the etiology is not established.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 24696751

name: Intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm with associated invasive carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Invasive carcinoma arising from an intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasm, a defined precursor lesion of the pancreatic ducts; IPMNs are largely sporadic (KRAS/GNAS driven) but risk is increased in familial pancreatic cancer settings such as Peutz-Jeghers syndrome, with the precursor lesion itself being the established route to invasion.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 26658770

name: Mucinous cystic neoplasm of the pancreas with associated invasive carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed, Digestive System
causes: Invasive carcinoma arising from a mucinous cystic neoplasm, a precursor lesion occurring almost exclusively in women and characterized by an ovarian-type stroma; the precursor cyst is the established route to invasion, but no external etiologic exposure is established for the neoplasm itself.
cause_source: WHO 5th ed; PMID 25723112

name: Adult granulosa cell tumor of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Cancer Research UK sex cord stromal ovarian tumours
causes: No established external cause; nearly all harbor a somatic FOXL2 C134W mutation, which is a molecular hallmark rather than an etiologic exposure, so the underlying cause is not established.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Shah et al. NEJM 2009

name: Juvenile granulosa cell tumor of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines ovarian sex cord stromal tumours
causes: Mostly sporadic with no established cause; a small subset is associated with enchondromatosis (Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome), and some cases carry AKT1 or GNAS alterations that are not etiologic exposures.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Sertoli-Leydig cell tumor of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; PMID sex cord stromal DICER1 FOXL2
causes: A substantial subset arises in the DICER1 tumor predisposition syndrome (germline DICER1 mutation), the main established predisposing factor; sporadic cases have no other established cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Schultz et al. Gynecol Oncol 2017

name: Ovarian dysgerminoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Diagnostic Histopathology ovarian germ cell tumours 2020
causes: Largely of unknown cause, but incidence is increased in dysgenetic gonads such as gonadal dysgenesis and androgen insensitivity with a Y chromosome (via gonadoblastoma as a precursor); most cases in normal ovaries are idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Ovarian Germ Cell Tumors

name: Ovarian yolk sac tumor
aliases: endodermal sinus tumor of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines ovary yolk sac tumor
causes: Sporadic malignant germ cell tumor with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause in most cases.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Immature teratoma of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Diagnostic Histopathology ovarian germ cell tumours 2020
causes: Sporadic germ cell tumor of young women with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Low-grade serous carcinoma of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Int J Gynecol Cancer rare ovarian tumors update 2024
causes: Arises through a stepwise pathway from serous borderline (atypical proliferative) tumors as a precursor lesion; established risk factors overlap with high-grade serous cancer, but no single external cause is defined and germline BRCA involvement is uncommon.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Vang et al. Adv Anat Pathol 2009

name: Mucinous carcinoma of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Int J Gynecol Cancer rare ovarian tumors update 2024
causes: Frequently arises through a benign to borderline to carcinoma sequence within mucinous cystadenomas and borderline tumors as precursor lesions; no established external cause, and smoking is a weak reported risk factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Endometrioid carcinoma of the ovary
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020
causes: Strongly associated with endometriosis as a precursor lesion; a subset arises in Lynch syndrome (mismatch repair gene) carriers, and unopposed estrogen exposure is a contributing factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: High-grade serous carcinoma of the fallopian tube
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Pathology Outlines serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma
causes: Arises from serous tubal intraepithelial carcinoma (STIC) in the fallopian tube fimbria as the precursor lesion, with a strong hereditary component from germline BRCA1 and BRCA2 mutations (and other homologous recombination genes).
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Lee et al. J Pathol 2007

name: Vulvar melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Cancer Research UK types of vulval cancer
causes: A mucosal or acral-type melanoma of largely unknown cause; unlike sun-exposed cutaneous melanoma it is not UV driven, and no established external cause is defined for most cases.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Vulvar Cancer

name: Extramammary Paget disease of the vulva
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818
causes: Usually a primary intraepithelial adnexal (apocrine) neoplasm of unknown cause, though a minority is secondary to an underlying anorectal or urothelial adenocarcinoma; no established external etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the vagina
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Vaginal Adenocarcinoma review PMC12249244
causes: Classically caused by in utero exposure to diethylstilbestrol (DES), which produced vaginal adenosis as the precursor; non-DES sporadic cases also occur and have no established cause.
cause_source: Herbst et al. NEJM 1971; WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Vaginal melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818
causes: A mucosal melanoma of largely unknown cause, not UV related; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Vaginal Cancer

name: Vaginal squamous cell carcinoma
aliases: squamous cell carcinoma of the vagina
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; comprehensive management of vulvovaginal cancers PMC12432818
causes: Most cases are caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16) through vaginal intraepithelial neoplasia as the precursor; additional risk from prior cervical HPV disease, smoking, and pelvic radiation.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Vaginal Cancer

name: Uterine serous carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; endometrial carcinoma changes to classification WHO 2020
causes: A high-grade endometrial carcinoma usually arising in atrophic endometrium via serous endometrial intraepithelial carcinoma as the precursor; associated with older age, and a subset relates to germline TP53 (Li-Fraumeni) and tamoxifen or prior pelvic radiation exposure. Most cases have no single external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Uterine clear cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; endometrial carcinoma changes to classification WHO 2020
causes: A high-grade endometrial carcinoma of largely unknown cause; associations include prior pelvic radiation and tamoxifen use, but most cases are sporadic with no established external etiology.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Low-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; JAZF1/SUZ12 gene fusion in endometrial stromal sarcomas PMC4754953
causes: Sporadic uterine mesenchymal tumor driven by recurrent gene fusions (commonly JAZF1::SUZ12); no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause, and prior pelvic radiation or tamoxifen are only weakly reported.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: High-grade endometrial stromal sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; molecular classification of endometrial stromal sarcomas PMC7563240
causes: Sporadic uterine sarcoma defined by fusions such as YWHAE::NUTM2 or BCOR alterations; no established external, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Uterine adenosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; Uterine Sarcoma Treatment NCI PDQ
causes: Mostly sporadic with no established cause; recognized risk factors include prior pelvic radiation, tamoxifen use, and long-term unopposed estrogen exposure.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma of the uterine corpus
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; mesonephric-like adenocarcinoma of the uterine corpus PMC8625485
causes: Cause not established; thought to arise from Mullerian epithelium showing mesonephric differentiation (or from mesonephric remnants) driven by KRAS and PIK3CA mutations, with no known environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Euscher et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2020

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix UpToDate
causes: Caused by high-risk human papillomavirus, predominantly HPV18 (and HPV16), the same infectious etiology as other cervical carcinomas.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Castle et al. Gynecol Oncol 2018

name: Large cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the cervix
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; MD Anderson small and large cell cervical cancer
causes: Caused by high-risk human papillomavirus infection (commonly HPV18 and HPV16), the same infectious etiology as other cervical carcinomas.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the cervix
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; clear cell adenocarcinoma of the cervix PMC7243845
causes: A subset is caused by in utero diethylstilbestrol (DES) exposure, which produced cervicovaginal adenosis; most contemporary cases are HPV-independent and sporadic with no established cause.
cause_source: Herbst et al. NEJM 1971; WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the cervix
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gastric-type adenocarcinoma of the uterine cervix PMC11075765
causes: An HPV-independent cervical adenocarcinoma; a subset arises in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (germline STK11 mutation), and lobular endocervical glandular hyperplasia is a recognized precursor lesion. Most cases are sporadic with somatic STK11 or TP53 alterations.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; Kojima et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2007

name: Mesonephric adenocarcinoma of the cervix
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; histological classification of cervical adenocarcinomas Yasuda 2025
causes: Arises from embryonic mesonephric (Wolffian) duct remnants in the cervix as its cell of origin; HPV-independent, with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Gestational choriocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic tumors review Arch Pathol Lab Med
causes: Arises from trophoblast of an antecedent pregnancy, most often following a complete hydatidiform mole but also after normal pregnancy, abortion, or ectopic pregnancy; a prior molar pregnancy is the principal predisposing event.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Gestational Trophoblastic Disease

name: Placental site trophoblastic tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; review of placental site trophoblastic tumors PMC6893905
causes: Arises from intermediate (implantation site) trophoblast of a prior gestation, typically months to years after a term pregnancy, non-molar abortion, or (less often) a hydatidiform mole; the antecedent pregnancy is the defining predisposing event.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Epithelioid trophoblastic tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic tumors review Arch Pathol Lab Med
causes: Arises from chorionic-type intermediate trophoblast of a prior gestation (term pregnancy, abortion, or molar pregnancy), which is the required predisposing event.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Invasive hydatidiform mole
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Female Genital Tumours 2020; gestational trophoblastic disease Dis Model Mech 2025
causes: A molar pregnancy (typically a complete hydatidiform mole from abnormal androgenetic fertilization) that invades the myometrium; it is by definition a complication of a preceding hydatidiform mole.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Female Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Gestational Trophoblastic Disease

name: Collecting duct carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Pathology Outlines kidney collecting duct carcinoma
causes: Aggressive renal carcinoma arising from the collecting ducts of Bellini with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause; it is sporadic and idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: TFE3-rearranged renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Some cases are associated with prior childhood cytotoxic chemotherapy exposure, a recognized predisposing factor; otherwise the tumor is sporadic and driven by TFE3 gene fusions that are not themselves an etiologic exposure.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Argani et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2006

name: TFEB-altered renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Sporadic MiT family translocation renal cell carcinoma driven by TFEB fusion or amplification; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Mucinous tubular and spindle cell carcinoma of the kidney
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; mucinous tubular and spindle cell RCC review PMC4573286
causes: Sporadic low-grade renal carcinoma with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Succinate dehydrogenase-deficient renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; SDH-deficient RCC characterization PMID 25034258
causes: Caused by germline mutations in a succinate dehydrogenase subunit gene (most often SDHB), an inherited predisposition that also causes paraganglioma and pheochromocytoma; biallelic SDH loss defines the tumor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Gill et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2014

name: Fumarate hydratase-deficient renal cell carcinoma
aliases: hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell carcinoma-associated RCC
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Caused by germline fumarate hydratase (FH) mutation defining hereditary leiomyomatosis and renal cell cancer (HLRCC) syndrome; the inherited FH defect is the predisposing cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Tomlinson et al. Nat Genet 2002

name: ELOC-mutated renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Sporadic renal cell carcinoma defined by biallelic somatic ELOC (TCEB1) inactivation; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause, and the somatic mutation is not an etiologic exposure.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Hakimi et al. Eur Urol 2015

name: ALK-rearranged renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Sporadic renal carcinoma driven by ALK gene fusions; some cases occur with sickle cell trait, but no consistent environmental, infectious, or clearly established hereditary cause is defined.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Tubulocystic renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Sporadic low-grade renal carcinoma with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Acquired cystic disease-associated renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Caused by end-stage renal disease with long-term dialysis and acquired cystic kidney disease, which is the defining predisposing condition; risk rises with dialysis duration.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Tickoo et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2006

name: Eosinophilic solid and cystic renal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 2022 classification of renal neoplasms Cureus
causes: Most cases are sporadic with somatic biallelic loss of TSC1 or TSC2, but a subset arises in patients with germline tuberous sclerosis complex, the established predisposing syndrome for these tumors.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Trpkov et al. Am J Surg Pathol 2019

name: Rhabdoid tumor of the kidney
aliases: malignant rhabdoid tumor of the kidney
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Wilms Tumor and Other Childhood Kidney Tumors NCI PDQ NBK65953
causes: Caused by biallelic inactivation of SMARCB1 (INI1), often with a germline mutation defining rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome; the inherited SWI/SNF defect is the recognized predisposing cause in a subset.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Paediatric Tumours; Versteege et al. Nature 1998

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the urinary bladder
aliases: small cell carcinoma of the bladder
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022
causes: Strongly associated with cigarette smoking, the same major carcinogen as urothelial carcinoma, and often coexists with urothelial carcinoma; it likely shares urothelial carcinogen exposures.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Urachal adenocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022
causes: Arises from persistent urachal remnants (the embryonic allantoic duct) at the bladder dome, with glandular or intestinal metaplasia as a precursor; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Plasmacytoid urothelial carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; histologic variants of urothelial carcinoma Alderson 2020
causes: An aggressive urothelial carcinoma variant defined by CDH1 (E-cadherin) loss; it shares urothelial carcinogen exposure, chiefly cigarette smoking, as the recognized risk factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Micropapillary urothelial carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; histologic variants of urothelial carcinoma Alderson 2020
causes: A urothelial carcinoma variant sharing established urothelial risk factors, principally cigarette smoking and occupational aromatic amine exposure.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Sarcomatoid urothelial carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; recent advances in classification of bladder cancer Guo 2022
causes: A high-grade urothelial carcinoma variant sharing standard bladder cancer risk factors (cigarette smoking, occupational aromatic amine exposure); prior cyclophosphamide or pelvic radiation are additional recognized risks.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Penile squamous cell carcinoma
aliases: squamous cell carcinoma of the penis
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; reclassification of penile SCC WHO 2022 PMC12231722
causes: About half are caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16); non-HPV cases are driven by chronic inflammation, phimosis, lichen sclerosus, poor hygiene, and smoking, with lack of circumcision as a risk factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Penile Cancer

name: Urethral carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; clear cell adenocarcinoma of urethra PMC10789969
causes: A subset is caused by high-risk human papillomavirus (chiefly HPV16), while other cases relate to chronic inflammation, urethral stricture, recurrent infection, and (in bulbar or proximal tumors) prior irritation; smoking is a contributing factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; NCI PDQ Urethral Cancer

name: Clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra
aliases: Mullerian-type clear cell adenocarcinoma of the urethra
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; primary urethral clear-cell adenocarcinoma PMC3936309
causes: Rare urethral adenocarcinoma often thought to arise from urethral diverticulum or nephrogenic adenosis (metaplasia) as a possible precursor; no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Spermatocytic tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657
causes: A germ cell tumor of older men arising from spermatogonia with no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary cause; it is not associated with cryptorchidism or germ cell neoplasia in situ.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Testicular Leydig cell tumor
aliases: Leydig cell tumour of the testis
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657
causes: Mostly sporadic sex cord-stromal tumor with no established external cause; rare cases occur in Klinefelter syndrome, and recurrent somatic activating mutations are not etiologic exposures.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Testicular Sertoli cell tumor
aliases: Sertoli cell tumour of the testis
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition classification of testicular tumours PMC9544657
causes: Mostly sporadic, but distinct subtypes arise in tumor predisposition syndromes: large cell calcifying Sertoli cell tumor in Carney complex (PRKAR1A) and intratubular large cell hyalinizing type in Peutz-Jeghers syndrome (STK11). Ordinary cases have no established cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Adenocarcinoma of the rete testis
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; Pathology Outlines testis and paratestis WHO classification
causes: Cause not established; it is extremely rare and idiopathic, with rete testis adenomatous hyperplasia proposed as a precursor and only unproven associations with hydrocele, chronic inflammation, and chemical exposures.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Sanchez-Chapado et al. review

name: Small cell neuroendocrine carcinoma of the prostate
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; neuroendocrine differentiation in prostate neoplasms PMC12695988
causes: Most cases arise as a treatment-emergent lineage transformation of prostatic adenocarcinoma under prolonged androgen deprivation and androgen receptor targeted therapy, the principal predisposing exposure; rare de novo cases have no established cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed; Aggarwal et al. J Clin Oncol 2018

name: Ductal adenocarcinoma of the prostate
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 2022; WHO 5th edition prostate cancer classification Kench 2022
causes: A prostatic adenocarcinoma variant arising from prostatic ducts; it shares the risk factors of acinar prostate cancer (age, family history, germline BRCA2 and other DNA repair gene mutations) with no distinct additional external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Urinary and Male Genital Tumours 5th ed

name: Porocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; YAP1 fusions (Sekine 2019 J Clin Invest)
causes: Frequently arises by malignant transformation of a preexisting benign poroma (eccrine sweat gland tumor); prior radiation, chronic sun exposure, and immunosuppression are reported risk factors, though many cases develop de novo.
cause_source: Salih et al., J Cutan Pathol reviews; Robson et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2001

name: Hidradenocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet
causes: Can arise by malignant transformation of a preexisting benign hidradenoma (sweat gland tumor), but most cases have no established external cause and occur sporadically.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 2018; Gauerke & Driscoll, Arch Pathol Lab Med 2010

name: Spiradenocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; arises in CYLD-mutant lesions
causes: Classically arises by malignant transformation of a long-standing benign spiradenoma or spiradenocylindroma, sometimes in the setting of Brooke-Spiegler syndrome (germline CYLD); otherwise no established environmental cause.
cause_source: Kazakov et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2009; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Microcystic adnexal carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); SEER incidence ~0.5 per million (Uptodate/Medscape)
causes: Cause is not firmly established, but the strongest associations are prior therapeutic or ionizing radiation to the site (often with decades of latency) and chronic UV/sun exposure of the head and neck; immunosuppression is a possible contributor.
cause_source: Yu et al., Br J Dermatol reviews; UpToDate Microcystic adnexal carcinoma

name: Trichilemmal carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet
causes: Generally arises on chronically sun-damaged skin of elderly patients, implicating cumulative UV exposure; it is otherwise sporadic with no established hereditary cause and follows an indolent course.
cause_source: Hamman et al., Dermatol Surg reviews; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Pilomatrix carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet
causes: Cause is largely unknown; the malignant counterpart of pilomatricoma, it is driven by activating CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutations but has no established environmental or hereditary etiologic factor, and most cases are sporadic.
cause_source: Lazar et al./Hardisson et al., Am J Dermatopathol 2001; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Apocrine carcinoma of skin
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet
causes: Cause is unknown; this rare sweat gland malignancy arises most often in apocrine-rich areas (axilla, anogenital region) but has no established environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiology, and most cases are sporadic.
cause_source: Robson et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2008; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Eccrine carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet
causes: Cause is largely unknown; this heterogeneous group of malignant eccrine sweat gland tumors is mostly sporadic, with no consistently established environmental or hereditary cause, although radiation and immunosuppression are occasionally implicated.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 2018; Wildemore et al., Dermatol Surg reviews

name: Digital papillary adenocarcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; HPV42-associated (Leiendecker 2023 Cancer Discov)
causes: Cause is unknown; this aggressive sweat gland carcinoma characteristically arises on the fingers, palms, toes, and soles, but no environmental, occupational, or hereditary etiologic factor has been established and cases are sporadic.
cause_source: Duke et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2000; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Endocrine mucin-producing sweat gland carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); systematic review 110 cases (Abdulkader 2021 J Dermatolog Treat, PMID 34057875)
causes: Cause is not established; this low-grade periocular sweat gland carcinoma, thought to arise from apocrine glands and considered a precursor of some primary cutaneous mucinous carcinomas, occurs sporadically in older adults with no identified risk factor.
cause_source: Zembowicz et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2005; PathologyOutlines EMPSGC

name: Primary cutaneous mucinous carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); incidence <0.1 per million (PathologyOutlines; PMC3481811)
causes: Cause is unknown; this rare low-grade sweat gland carcinoma (some cases evolving from endocrine mucin-producing sweat gland carcinoma) occurs sporadically, most often on the head and neck of older adults, with no established etiologic factor.
cause_source: Kazakov et al., Am J Surg Pathol reviews; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Primary cutaneous adenoid cystic carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; MYB-NFIB fusion
causes: Cause is unknown; this rare cutaneous adnexal carcinoma occurs sporadically, chiefly on the scalp and chest of adults, and no environmental, infectious, or hereditary etiologic factor has been established.
cause_source: Naylor et al., J Cutan Pathol reviews; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Primary cutaneous cribriform carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); downgraded to cribriform tumor in 5th ed
causes: Cause is unknown; consensus on etiology has not been reached for this rare low-grade cutaneous apocrine (cribriform) carcinoma, which arises sporadically on the extremities with no identified environmental or hereditary risk factor.
cause_source: Rutten et al., reappraisal - Arch Dermatol Res 2022; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Extramammary Paget disease
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; HER2 in 30-60% (PMC7761697)
causes: Most cases are primary intraepithelial adenocarcinomas of unknown cause arising in apocrine-gland-rich skin (vulva, perianal, scrotal); a minority are secondary, representing epidermal spread from an underlying regional adenocarcinoma (for example anorectal or urothelial), which is the identifiable cause in those cases.
cause_source: Lopes Filho et al., An Bras Dermatol 2015; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Atypical fibroxanthoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); UV-induced TP53 mutations (PMID 32540221)
causes: Arises on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck in elderly patients and carries UV-signature TP53 mutations, implicating cumulative UV exposure; prior radiation and immunosuppression (organ transplant) are additional established risk factors.
cause_source: Griewank et al., Mod Pathol 2018; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Pleomorphic dermal sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); recurrent UV-induced TP53, PIK3CA, RAS (PMID 32540221)
causes: Occurs on severely sun-damaged skin of the head and neck in elderly patients and harbors UV-induced TP53 driver mutations, implicating chronic UV exposure; prior radiation and immunosuppression are additional risk factors, and it is a more aggressive relative of atypical fibroxanthoma.
cause_source: Griewank et al., Mod Pathol 2018; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Cutaneous angiosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Orphanet; MYC amplification in radiation/lymphedema-associated
causes: Two well-established etiologic settings account for many cases: prior therapeutic radiation (for example post-breast-radiation chest wall angiosarcoma) and chronic lymphedema (Stewart-Treves syndrome); the remaining idiopathic scalp/face angiosarcomas of the elderly are linked to chronic sun exposure.
cause_source: Young et al., Lancet Oncol 2010; Stewart & Treves, Cancer 1948

name: Advanced basal cell carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); Hedgehog pathway PTCH1/SMO (NEJM 2009 Von Hoff); metastasis rate <0.5%
causes: Driven overwhelmingly by chronic UV/sun exposure activating the Hedgehog pathway (PTCH1/SMO); additional causes include prior radiation, arsenic exposure, immunosuppression, and germline PTCH1 mutations in Gorlin (nevoid basal cell carcinoma) syndrome.
cause_source: Sekulic et al., N Engl J Med 2012; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Desmoplastic melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); NF1 in >50% (PMID 26076063); NFKBIE promoter (Nat Genet 2015)
causes: Strongly linked to cumulative UV exposure, arising on chronically sun-damaged skin of the head and neck of older adults and carrying one of the highest UV mutational burdens of any melanoma subtype.
cause_source: Shain et al., Nat Genet 2015; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Conjunctival melanoma
source: WHO Classification; Orphanet; BRAF ~35% (PMID 27009410); incidence ~0.5 per million
causes: Often arises from preexisting primary acquired melanosis (conjunctival melanocytic intraepithelial neoplasia) as a precursor lesion; UV/sun exposure is a proposed contributing factor, and it carries UV-signature and BRAF mutations in a subset.
cause_source: Shields et al., Ophthalmology 2011; Larsen et al., Invest Ophthalmol Vis Sci 2016

name: Spitz melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); kinase fusions ROS1/ALK/NTRK1/RET/BRAF (Lab Invest 2017)
causes: Cause is unknown; this melanoma with Spitzoid morphology is defined by kinase gene fusions (for example ALK, ROS1, NTRK1, BRAF) rather than UV damage, shows a low UV mutational burden, and has no established environmental or hereditary etiologic factor.
cause_source: Wiesner et al., Nat Commun 2014; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 2018

name: Lentigo maligna melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); chronic sun-damage (CSD) pathway
causes: Directly caused by long-term cumulative UV/sun exposure, developing from a lentigo maligna (in situ) precursor on chronically sun-damaged skin of the face and head in older adults.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 2018; Shain & Bastian, Nat Rev Cancer 2016

name: Nevoid melanoma
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023)
causes: Cause is not specifically established; this rare melanoma that histologically mimics a benign nevus occurs sporadically, and no distinct etiologic factor beyond the general melanoma risk factors (including UV exposure) has been defined for the subtype.
cause_source: Zembowicz & Scolyer, Arch Pathol Lab Med 2011; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Melanoma arising in blue nevus
source: WHO Classification of Skin Tumours 5th ed (2023); GNAQ/GNA11, BAP1 loss
causes: Arises by malignant transformation of a preexisting cellular blue nevus (the identifiable precursor lesion); it is driven by GNAQ/GNA11 mutations, is not UV-related, and has no established environmental cause otherwise.
cause_source: Costa et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2016; WHO Classification of Skin Tumours

name: Poorly differentiated thyroid carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed (Turin criteria); RAS/BRAF/TP53/CTNNB1 (PMC7286943)
causes: Typically arises by progression/dedifferentiation from a preexisting well-differentiated papillary or follicular thyroid carcinoma; prior radiation exposure and long-standing goiter are contributing risk factors, with RAS, TP53, and TERT promoter mutations accumulating during progression.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Endocrine Tumours 2022; Volante et al., Endocr Pathol 2021

name: Parathyroid carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; incidence ~3.5-5.7 per 10 million (SEER PMC8946517); CDC73/HRPT2
causes: Most cases are driven by inactivation of the CDC73 (HRPT2) gene, occurring somatically in sporadic tumors or as a germline mutation in hyperparathyroidism-jaw tumor syndrome; MEN1 and prior neck irradiation are additional predisposing factors.
cause_source: Shattuck et al., N Engl J Med 2003 (HRPT2/CDC73); WHO Classification of Endocrine Tumours

name: Pituitary carcinoma
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; ~0.2% of pituitary tumors (PMC9493437); defined by craniospinal/distant metastasis
causes: Cause is largely unknown; these very rare tumors nearly always arise by malignant progression from a preexisting aggressive pituitary adenoma/neuroendocrine tumor rather than de novo, and no consistent environmental or hereditary etiology has been established (rare cases occur in MEN1 or AIP-related settings).
cause_source: Raverot et al., Eur J Endocrinol 2018 (ESE Guidelines); WHO Classification of Endocrine Tumours

name: Aggressive pituitary neuroendocrine tumor
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; ~0.5% of adenomas display aggressive course (PMC7303064)
causes: Cause is largely unknown; most arise sporadically, with a minority linked to hereditary syndromes such as MEN1 (germline MEN1), Carney complex (PRKAR1A), and AIP-related familial isolated pituitary adenoma; no environmental cause is established.
cause_source: Raverot et al., Eur J Endocrinol 2018; WHO Classification of Endocrine Tumours 2022

name: Well-differentiated neuroendocrine tumor of the pancreas
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; MEN1-associated; incidence <1 per 100,000 (RARECAREnet)
causes: Most are sporadic with no established cause; a minority arise in hereditary syndromes, notably MEN1 (germline MEN1), von Hippel-Lindau disease (VHL), neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1), and tuberous sclerosis (TSC1/TSC2).
cause_source: Jensen et al., Neuroendocrinology 2012; WHO Classification of Endocrine Tumours

name: Extra-adrenal retroperitoneal paraganglioma
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; SDHB confers highest metastatic risk (PMC10029328)
causes: Strongly hereditary, most commonly driven by germline SDHx mutations (especially SDHB, which predisposes to extra-adrenal and malignant paragangliomas), along with VHL, NF1, and other susceptibility genes; the remainder are sporadic.
cause_source: Fishbein & Nathanson, Cancer Genet 2012; Lenders et al., J Clin Endocrinol Metab 2014

name: Carotid body paraganglioma
source: WHO Classification of Endocrine and Neuroendocrine Tumours 5th ed; Orphanet; SDHD germline (head and neck paraganglioma)
causes: Caused both by germline SDHx mutations (classically SDHD in hereditary paraganglioma type 1, also SDHB/SDHC) and by chronic hypoxic stimulation of the carotid body, as in long-term high-altitude residence or chronic hypoxemic lung disease.
cause_source: Baysal et al., Science 2000 (SDHD); Cerecer-Gil et al., Clin Cancer Res 2010 (altitude/SDHB)

name: Clear cell sarcoma of soft tissue
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining EWSR1::ATF1 (or EWSR1::CREB1) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant Triton tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: This is a malignant peripheral nerve sheath tumor with rhabdomyoblastic differentiation; about half of cases arise in patients with neurofibromatosis type 1 (germline NF1), and prior therapeutic radiation is also an established risk factor.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020); Frontiers Oncol 2014 MPNST review (PMC4233912)

name: Pseudomyogenic hemangioendothelioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining SERPINE1::FOSB (or ACTB::FOSB) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Retiform hemangioendothelioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a rare low-grade vascular tumor with no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Composite hemangioendothelioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a rare low-grade vascular tumor with no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Papillary intralymphatic angioendothelioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically (Dabska tumor) as a rare low-grade vascular tumor with no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Well-differentiated liposarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The characteristic MDM2/CDK4 amplification on supernumerary ring or giant marker chromosomes is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Dedifferentiated liposarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The characteristic MDM2/CDK4 amplification is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Pleomorphic liposarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically with complex genomic changes and no defining fusion, and no consistent inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Myxoid pleomorphic liposarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a rare aggressive liposarcoma of children and young adults, with reported occurrence in Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53) but most cases having no identified inherited cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Spindle cell/sclerosing rhabdomyosarcoma
key_gene_or_marker: MYOD1 p.L122R; VGLL2/NCOA2 fusions (infantile); TFCP2/MEIS1 fusions
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. It is driven by somatic events (for example VGLL2/NCOA2 fusions in infants or MYOD1 mutation in adults), which are acquired, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Ectomesenchymoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a rare pediatric tumor with rhabdomyoblastic and neuronal elements and no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant solitary fibrous tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining NAB2::STAT6 fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Epithelioid inflammatory myofibroblastic sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. It is driven by somatic ALK gene rearrangement (often RANBP2::ALK), which is an acquired event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant perivascular epithelioid cell tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Most cases are sporadic with no established cause, but a subset arises in the tuberous sclerosis complex (germline TSC1 or TSC2 mutation), consistent with the recurrent somatic TSC pathway alterations seen in these tumors.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Adult fibrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a diagnosis of exclusion with no defining genetic alteration and no consistent inherited or environmental cause; prior therapeutic radiation can rarely precede fibrosarcoma at an irradiated site.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Infantile fibrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically in infants. The defining ETV6::NTRK3 fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Myxoinflammatory fibroblastic sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically, typically in the distal extremities, with recurrent somatic TGFBR3/MGEA5 rearrangement or BRAF alteration that is acquired, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Low-grade fibromyxoid sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining FUS::CREB3L2 (or FUS::CREB3L1) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Sclerosing epithelioid fibrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The recurrent EWSR1::CREB3L1 (or FUS::CREB3L2) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Extraskeletal Ewing sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining EWSR1::FLI1 (or EWSR1::ERG) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: NTRK-rearranged spindle cell neoplasm
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining NTRK1/2/3 gene rearrangement is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant granular cell tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically, usually as a rare malignant counterpart of the Schwann cell derived granular cell tumor, with no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant glomus tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Most cases are sporadic with no established cause, though multiple/familial glomus tumors can occur with germline glomulin (GLMN) mutation; malignant transformation itself has no recognized inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant ossifying fibromyxoid tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically with recurrent somatic PHF1 gene rearrangement that is acquired, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant phosphaturic mesenchymal tumor
aliases: Malignant phosphaturic mesenchymal tumor mixed connective tissue variant
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. Most harbor a somatic FN1::FGFR1 (or FN1::FGF1) fusion driving FGF23 secretion, which is an acquired event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Intimal sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically within large blood vessels or the heart, with recurrent somatic MDM2 amplification that is acquired, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Desmoid-type fibromatosis
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Most cases are sporadic driven by somatic CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutation, but a recognized subset arises in familial adenomatous polyposis (germline APC mutation, Gardner syndrome); pregnancy, prior trauma, and surgery are reported associations.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020); GeneReviews APC-Associated Polyposis Conditions

name: CIC-rearranged sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining CIC::DUX4 (or other CIC) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Sarcoma with BCOR genetic alteration
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining BCOR alteration (BCOR::CCNB3 fusion or BCOR internal tandem duplication) is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Round cell sarcoma with EWSR1-non-ETS fusion
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining EWSR1 (or FUS) fusion to a non-ETS partner such as NFATC2 or PATZ1 is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Extrarenal rhabdoid tumor
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Most cases are sporadic driven by biallelic somatic SMARCB1 (INI1) inactivation, but a substantial subset arises in the rhabdoid tumor predisposition syndrome from a germline SMARCB1 (or rarely SMARCA4) mutation.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020); GeneReviews Rhabdoid Tumor Predisposition Syndrome

name: Undifferentiated round cell sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as an undifferentiated small round cell sarcoma lacking a defining recurrent alteration and with no consistent inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Undifferentiated spindle cell sarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a diagnosis of exclusion with no defining alteration and no consistent inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant tenosynovial giant cell tumor
aliases: Malignant giant cell tumor of tendon sheath
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically as a rare malignant counterpart of tenosynovial giant cell tumor, typically with somatic CSF1 rearrangement, which is an acquired event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Angiomatoid fibrous histiocytoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically. The defining EWSR1::CREB1 (or EWSR1::ATF1 / FUS::ATF1) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Malignant myoepithelioma of soft tissue
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically, frequently with somatic EWSR1 (or FUS) gene rearrangement, which is an acquired event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Extraskeletal osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet; NCI
causes: Most cases are sporadic with complex genomic changes and no established cause, but prior therapeutic radiation to the site is a recognized risk factor for a subset of extraskeletal osteosarcomas.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020); NCI PDQ Adult Soft Tissue Sarcoma

name: Biphenotypic sinonasal sarcoma
aliases: Low-grade sinonasal sarcoma with neural and myogenic features
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed Soft Tissue & Bone; Orphanet
causes: Cause not established; arises sporadically in the sinonasal tract. The defining PAX3::MAML3 (or other PAX3) fusion is a somatic event, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Soft Tissue and Bone Tumours (5th ed, 2020)

name: Conventional osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Most cases are sporadic, but established risk factors include hereditary retinoblastoma (germline RB1), Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53), Rothmund-Thomson and other RECQL-helicase syndromes, prior therapeutic radiation, and rapid bone growth in adolescence.
cause_source: Mirabello et al., Cancer 2009; WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Telangiectatic osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare high-grade osteosarcoma variant that is generally sporadic but shares the osteosarcoma predisposition context (hereditary RB1 retinoblastoma, Li-Fraumeni TP53, and prior radiation); no distinct environmental cause is established for this subtype.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Small cell osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare osteosarcoma variant that is generally sporadic; it falls within the broader osteosarcoma risk context (hereditary RB1, Li-Fraumeni TP53, prior radiation) but has no established subtype-specific cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Low-grade central osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare intramedullary low-grade osteosarcoma characterized by MDM2 amplification; it is sporadic with no established environmental cause or defined hereditary predisposition.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Dujardin et al., Mod Pathol 2011

name: Parosteal osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A low-grade surface osteosarcoma defined by MDM2/CDK4 amplification (supernumerary ring chromosomes); it is sporadic with no established environmental or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Periosteal osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: An intermediate-grade chondroblastic surface osteosarcoma that is sporadic; no established environmental exposure or hereditary predisposition is defined for this subtype.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: High-grade surface osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare high-grade osteosarcoma arising on the bone surface; it is generally sporadic within the broader osteosarcoma risk context (RB1, TP53, radiation) and has no established subtype-specific cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Secondary osteosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: By definition arises on a background of a predisposing condition, most commonly prior therapeutic or occupational radiation and Paget disease of bone in older adults, and also from bone infarcts, fibrous dysplasia, or chronic osteomyelitis.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Hansen et al., Semin Musculoskelet Radiol 2011

name: Conventional chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Most are sporadic and arise de novo, but a subset develops from benign precursor lesions, including secondary transformation of enchondromas (Ollier disease and Maffucci syndrome, driven by IDH1/IDH2 mutations) or osteochondromas (multiple osteochondromas, EXT1/EXT2).
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Amary et al., Nat Genet 2011

name: Dedifferentiated chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Arises when a low-grade cartilaginous tumor (often a preexisting or concurrent conventional chondrosarcoma or enchondroma) transforms to a high-grade nonchondroid sarcoma; it shares enchondromatosis-associated IDH mutation etiology but is otherwise sporadic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Mesenchymal chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare aggressive chondrosarcoma defined by a somatic HEY1::NCOA2 fusion; it is sporadic with no established environmental exposure or hereditary predisposition.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Wang et al., Genes Chromosomes Cancer 2012

name: Clear cell chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare low-grade chondrosarcoma of the epiphyses that is sporadic; no established environmental cause or hereditary predisposition is defined.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Periosteal chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare surface chondrosarcoma that is generally sporadic; no established environmental or hereditary cause is defined, though it belongs to the cartilaginous tumor spectrum that can associate with enchondromatosis.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Secondary peripheral chondrosarcoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Arises from malignant transformation of the cartilage cap of a preexisting osteochondroma, so multiple osteochondromas (hereditary EXT1/EXT2 mutations) is an established predisposing condition; solitary osteochondromas can also transform.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Bovee, Orphanet J Rare Dis 2008

name: Conventional chordoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020); RARECAREnet
causes: Arises from persistent notochordal remnants along the axial skeleton (clivus, spine, sacrum), often from benign notochordal cell tumors; rare familial chordoma is caused by germline duplication of the T (TBXT/brachyury) gene, and tuberous sclerosis is a rare predisposition.
cause_source: Yang et al., Nat Genet 2009; WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Poorly differentiated chordoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare aggressive chordoma variant defined by SMARCB1 (INI1) loss, arising from notochordal remnants; it is predominantly sporadic, though rare cases occur in patients with SMARCB1-related predisposition.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Hasselblatt et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2016

name: Dedifferentiated chordoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Arises when a conventional chordoma (from notochordal remnants) transforms into a high-grade sarcomatous component; it is sporadic and shares the notochordal origin of conventional chordoma, sometimes after prior treatment.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Malignant giant cell tumor of bone
aliases: Malignancy in giant cell tumor of bone
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Usually arises from malignant transformation of a conventional (H3F3A-mutant) giant cell tumor of bone, either as primary malignancy or, importantly, secondary malignancy after radiotherapy of a giant cell tumor; otherwise sporadic with no environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Adamantinoma of long bones
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare low-grade biphasic bone tumor of the tibia/fibula that shows a well-recognized clinicopathologic relationship with osteofibrous dysplasia (regarded as part of a spectrum/possible precursor), but it has no established environmental exposure or hereditary predisposition.
cause_source: Most et al., Clin Orthop Relat Res 2010; WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Dedifferentiated adamantinoma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Arises from high-grade transformation of a conventional adamantinoma of long bones; it is sporadic with no established environmental or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Undifferentiated high-grade pleomorphic sarcoma of bone
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Mostly a sporadic primary high-grade bone sarcoma, but a meaningful proportion are secondary, arising after prior radiation, in Paget disease of bone, or at sites of bone infarct; a diagnosis of exclusion after ruling out osteosarcoma.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Angiosarcoma of bone
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: Predominantly sporadic; a subset is secondary to prior radiation therapy or arises in bone infarcts or at sites of chronic injury, but no strong hereditary predisposition is established for the bone form.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed); Verbeke et al., Am J Surg Pathol 2011

name: Leiomyosarcoma of bone
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A rare primary smooth muscle sarcoma of bone that is largely sporadic; a subset is radiation-associated, and rare cases occur in Li-Fraumeni syndrome or in EBV-associated smooth muscle tumors of immunosuppressed patients, but no defined common cause exists.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Fibrosarcoma of bone
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Soft Tissue and Bone, 2020)
causes: A now-rare diagnosis of exclusion; most are sporadic primary bone tumors, though a subset is secondary to prior radiation, Paget disease of bone, or bone infarct, similar to other high-grade bone sarcomas.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Tumours: Soft Tissue and Bone (5th ed)

name: Medulloblastoma, WNT-activated
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: Mostly sporadic, but the WNT subgroup is enriched for germline APC mutations (familial adenomatous polyposis / Turcot syndrome); somatic CTNNB1 mutation is the usual driver in sporadic cases.
cause_source: Waszak et al., Lancet Oncol 2018; WHO CNS5 2021

name: Medulloblastoma, SHH-activated and TP53-wildtype
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: SHH-pathway medulloblastoma is associated with germline predisposition, notably Gorlin (nevoid basal cell carcinoma) syndrome from PTCH1 or SUFU mutations, and germline DICER1 or PTCH1 variants; many cases are still sporadic.
cause_source: Waszak et al., Lancet Oncol 2018; WHO CNS5 2021

name: Medulloblastoma, SHH-activated and TP53-mutant
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: This aggressive subgroup is strongly enriched for germline TP53 mutations (Li-Fraumeni syndrome), and some cases carry germline PTCH1 or SUFU variants; the TP53 mutation drives the SHH-activated phenotype.
cause_source: Zhukova et al., J Clin Oncol 2013; Waszak et al., Lancet Oncol 2018

name: Medulloblastoma, non-WNT/non-SHH
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: Comprises group 3 and group 4 medulloblastoma, which are largely sporadic with no established hereditary syndrome or environmental cause; driven by somatic events such as MYC amplification and structural chromosomal changes.
cause_source: Northcott et al., Nature 2017; WHO CNS5 2021

name: Embryonal tumor with multilayered rosettes
aliases: Embryonal tumour with multilayered rosettes, C19MC-altered
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: Most cases are driven by amplification of the C19MC microRNA cluster (chr19q13.42), a somatic event; a minority arise in the context of germline DICER1 pathogenic variants (DICER1 syndrome), and it is otherwise sporadic with no environmental cause.
cause_source: Kleinman et al., Nat Genet 2014; WHO CNS5 2021

name: CNS neuroblastoma, FOXR2-activated
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: A CNS embryonal tumor defined by somatic FOXR2 activation; it is sporadic with no established hereditary predisposition or environmental cause.
cause_source: Sturm et al., Cell 2016; WHO CNS5 2021

name: CNS tumor with BCOR internal tandem duplication
aliases: CNS tumour with BCOR internal tandem duplication
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: A CNS embryonal tumor defined by a somatic BCOR internal tandem duplication; it is sporadic with no established hereditary predisposition or environmental cause.
cause_source: Sturm et al., Cell 2016; WHO CNS5 2021

name: CNS embryonal tumor, not otherwise specified
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: A heterogeneous group of primitive CNS embryonal tumors lacking a defining molecular alteration; it is sporadic with no established environmental cause, though rare cases may occur with cancer predisposition syndromes.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 2021

name: Medulloepithelioma
source: WHO Classification of Tumours 5th ed (Central Nervous System, 2021)
causes: A rare embryonal CNS or intraocular tumor recapitulating primitive neural tube; most are sporadic, though intraocular (ciliary body) medulloepithelioma is associated with germline DICER1 pathogenic variants (DICER1 syndrome).
cause_source: Sahm et al., Acta Neuropathol 2016; WHO Classification of Tumours

name: Astrocytoma, isocitrate dehydrogenase-mutant
aliases: Diffuse astrocytoma IDH-mutant CNS WHO grade 2-4
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic with no established environmental cause; the defining IDH1 or IDH2 mutation is a somatic driver, not an inherited cause. Prior therapeutic ionizing radiation is an established risk factor for gliomas, and Li-Fraumeni syndrome (germline TP53) and constitutional mismatch repair deficiency predispose to gliomas in a minority.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; glioma predisposition literature (Li-Fraumeni, CMMRD, radiation)

name: Oligodendroglioma, isocitrate dehydrogenase-mutant and 1p/19q-codeleted
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic with no established cause; IDH mutation and 1p/19q codeletion are somatic defining events, not inherited causes. Prior therapeutic ionizing radiation is a recognized risk factor for gliomas in general.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; glioma radiation risk literature

name: Diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic pediatric high-grade glioma with no established cause; the defining H3 K27M (or EZHIP) alteration is a somatic driver, not a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (diffuse midline glioma, H3 K27-altered)

name: Diffuse hemispheric glioma, H3 G34-mutant
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic high-grade glioma of adolescents and young adults with no established cause; the defining H3 G34R/V mutation is a somatic event rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (diffuse hemispheric glioma, H3 G34-mutant)

name: Diffuse pediatric-type high-grade glioma, H3-wildtype and IDH-wildtype
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Largely sporadic with no established cause; a subset arises in the context of cancer predisposition syndromes such as constitutional mismatch repair deficiency, Li-Fraumeni syndrome, and neurofibromatosis type 1, and prior therapeutic radiation is a recognized risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; pediatric high-grade glioma predisposition literature (CMMRD, Li-Fraumeni, NF1)

name: Infant-type hemispheric glioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic infant glioma with no established cause; the recurrent receptor tyrosine kinase fusions (ALK, ROS1, NTRK, MET) are somatic drivers rather than inherited or environmental causes.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (infant-type hemispheric glioma)

name: Pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic tumor with no established cause; the frequent BRAF V600E mutation and CDKN2A/B loss are somatic events, not heritable or environmental causes.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (pleomorphic xanthoastrocytoma)

name: Subependymal giant cell astrocytoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Occurs almost exclusively in tuberous sclerosis complex, an autosomal dominant disorder from germline TSC1 or TSC2 mutation, with biallelic TSC inactivation driving the tumor; it is therefore a syndrome-defining lesion rather than a sporadic tumor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; SEGA/tuberous sclerosis TSC1/TSC2 literature (Oncotarget 2017)

name: Chordoid glioma
aliases: Chordoid glioma of the third ventricle
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor of the third ventricle with no established cause; the recurrent PRKCA D463H mutation is a somatic driver, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (chordoid glioma)

name: Astroblastoma, MN1-altered
aliases: High-grade neuroepithelial tumor with MN1 alteration
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
