causes: Rare sporadic tumor with no established cause; the defining MN1 gene rearrangement is a somatic alteration rather than a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (astroblastoma, MN1-altered)

name: Angiocentric glioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare benign sporadic glioma of childhood and young adults with no established cause; the characteristic MYB or MYB-QKI alteration is a somatic driver, not an inherited cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (angiocentric glioma)

name: High-grade astrocytoma with piloid features
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic tumor with no established cause; recurrent MAPK pathway alterations, CDKN2A/B loss, and ATRX changes are somatic events, although rare cases arise on a background of neurofibromatosis type 1.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (high-grade astrocytoma with piloid features)

name: Supratentorial ependymoma, ZFTA fusion-positive
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic ependymoma with no established cause; the defining ZFTA (formerly C11orf95) fusion, most often with RELA, is a somatic driver rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (supratentorial ependymoma, ZFTA fusion-positive)

name: Supratentorial ependymoma, YAP1 fusion-positive
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic ependymoma of young children with no established cause; the defining YAP1 fusion is a somatic driver, not a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (supratentorial ependymoma, YAP1 fusion-positive)

name: Posterior fossa ependymoma, group PFA
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic infant and childhood ependymoma with no established cause; it is defined by a CpG island hypermethylation and EZHIP overexpression epigenetic phenotype (a somatic feature), not by an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (posterior fossa ependymoma, group PFA)

name: Posterior fossa ependymoma, group PFB
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic posterior fossa ependymoma of older children and adults with no established cause; it is defined by its methylation and cytogenetic profile rather than a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (posterior fossa ependymoma, group PFB)

name: Spinal ependymoma, MYCN-amplified
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare aggressive spinal ependymoma with no established cause; the defining MYCN amplification is a somatic driver, not an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (spinal ependymoma, MYCN-amplified)

name: Myxopapillary ependymoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic tumor of the filum terminale/conus with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (myxopapillary ependymoma)

name: Subependymoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Benign, often incidental sporadic tumor with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (subependymoma)

name: Atypical choroid plexus papilloma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Mostly sporadic with no established environmental cause; germline TP53 mutation (Li-Fraumeni syndrome) predisposes to choroid plexus tumors and should be considered, particularly in children, although the syndrome link is strongest for choroid plexus carcinoma.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; choroid plexus tumor / Li-Fraumeni TP53 literature

name: Ganglioglioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic glioneuronal tumor with no established cause; the frequent BRAF V600E mutation is a somatic driver rather than a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (ganglioglioma)

name: Extraventricular neurocytoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor with no established cause and no recognized hereditary syndrome or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (extraventricular neurocytoma)

name: Cerebellar liponeurocytoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic cerebellar tumor of adults with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (cerebellar liponeurocytoma)

name: Rosette-forming glioneuronal tumor
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor, typically of the fourth ventricle, with no established cause; recurrent FGFR1 and PIK3CA/NF1 alterations are somatic events rather than inherited or environmental causes.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (rosette-forming glioneuronal tumor)

name: Papillary glioneuronal tumor
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic glioneuronal tumor with no established cause; the defining PRKCA fusion (typically SLC44A1-PRKCA) is a somatic driver, not a heritable or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (papillary glioneuronal tumor)

name: Myxoid glioneuronal tumor
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor with no established cause; the defining PDGFRA dinucleotide mutation is a somatic event rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (myxoid glioneuronal tumor)

name: Diffuse glioneuronal tumor with oligodendroglioma-like features and nuclear clusters
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare, recently described sporadic tumor with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (diffuse glioneuronal tumor with oligodendroglioma-like features and nuclear clusters)

name: Diffuse leptomeningeal glioneuronal tumor
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor with no established cause; recurrent KIAA1549-BRAF fusion combined with 1p deletion is a somatic feature rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (diffuse leptomeningeal glioneuronal tumor)

name: Pineal parenchymal tumor of intermediate differentiation
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic pineal tumor with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (pineal parenchymal tumor of intermediate differentiation)

name: Papillary tumor of the pineal region
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Rare sporadic tumor of the pineal region with no established cause and no recognized hereditary or environmental risk factor.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (papillary tumor of the pineal region)

name: Desmoplastic myxoid tumor of the pineal region, SMARCB1-mutant
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Very rare, recently described sporadic pineal tumor with no established cause; the SMARCB1 alteration reported to date is a somatic event rather than a proven inherited cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (desmoplastic myxoid tumor of the pineal region, SMARCB1-mutant)

name: Central nervous system yolk sac tumor
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Most intracranial germ cell tumors are sporadic, but Klinefelter syndrome and Down syndrome are established predisposing conditions for CNS germ cell tumors including yolk sac tumor.
cause_source: CNS germ cell tumor reviews (Klinefelter/Down associations)

name: Central nervous system embryonal carcinoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: A CNS germ cell tumor that is usually sporadic, but Klinefelter syndrome and Down syndrome are established predisposing conditions for intracranial germ cell tumors.
cause_source: CNS germ cell tumor reviews (Klinefelter/Down associations)

name: Central nervous system choriocarcinoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: A CNS germ cell tumor that is usually sporadic, but Klinefelter syndrome and Down syndrome are established predisposing conditions for intracranial germ cell tumors.
cause_source: CNS germ cell tumor reviews (Klinefelter/Down associations)

name: Central nervous system teratoma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: A CNS germ cell tumor that is usually sporadic, but Klinefelter syndrome and Down syndrome are established predisposing conditions for intracranial germ cell tumors.
cause_source: CNS germ cell tumor reviews (Klinefelter/Down associations)

name: Central nervous system mixed germ cell tumor
aliases: Mixed germ cell tumor of the CNS
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: A CNS germ cell tumor that is usually sporadic, but Klinefelter syndrome and Down syndrome are established predisposing conditions for intracranial germ cell tumors.
cause_source: CNS germ cell tumor reviews (Klinefelter/Down associations)

name: Atypical meningioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Most are sporadic, but prior therapeutic ionizing radiation is a well established risk factor and neurofibromatosis type 2 (germline NF2 mutation) predisposes to meningiomas including higher grade tumors.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; meningioma radiation and NF2 literature

name: Anaplastic meningioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Most are sporadic, but prior therapeutic ionizing radiation is a well established risk factor for meningioma and neurofibromatosis type 2 (germline NF2 mutation) predisposes to meningiomas including higher grade lesions.
cause_source: WHO CNS5; meningioma radiation and NF2 literature

name: Solitary fibrous tumor of the central nervous system
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic mesenchymal tumor with no established cause; the defining NAB2-STAT6 fusion is a somatic driver rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (solitary fibrous tumor)

name: Primary diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the central nervous system
aliases: Primary diffuse large B-cell lymphoma of the CNS
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: In immunodeficient patients (HIV/AIDS, transplant recipients, congenital immunodeficiency) it is caused by Epstein-Barr virus driven B-cell proliferation in the setting of immunosuppression; in immunocompetent patients it is largely sporadic with no established cause.
cause_source: WHO Haematolymphoid/CNS classifications; primary CNS lymphoma EBV/immunosuppression literature

name: Adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic tumor with no established cause; the defining somatic CTNNB1 (beta-catenin) mutation is a driver rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (adamantinomatous craniopharyngioma)

name: Papillary craniopharyngioma
source: WHO CNS5 (2021)
causes: Sporadic tumor of adults with no established cause; the defining somatic BRAF V600E mutation is a driver rather than an inherited or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO CNS5 (papillary craniopharyngioma)

name: Splenic B-cell lymphoma/leukemia with prominent nucleoli
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this rare splenic small B-cell neoplasm (formerly splenic diffuse red pulp lymphoma / hairy cell leukemia variant spectrum) is idiopathic with no confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: T-cell large granular lymphocytic leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established, but it is strongly linked to chronic immune stimulation and autoimmune disease, notably rheumatoid arthritis (overlapping with Felty syndrome), and is driven by acquired activating STAT3 mutations. No external etiologic agent is proven.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: NK-cell large granular lymphocytic leukemia
aliases: chronic lymphoproliferative disorder of NK cells
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this chronic NK-cell lymphoproliferation is idiopathic, associated with chronic immune activation and in some series with prior Epstein-Barr virus exposure, but no confirmed causal agent.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Aggressive NK-cell leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Strongly associated with Epstein-Barr virus, which is detected in nearly all cases, particularly in East Asian populations; EBV is regarded as central to its pathogenesis.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: T-prolymphocytic leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is driven by acquired chromosome 14 inversions/translocations activating TCL1 and by ATM inactivation. Patients with the hereditary ATM-deficiency disorder ataxia-telangiectasia are predisposed, but sporadic cases have no external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: B-prolymphocytic leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this rare B-cell leukemia (now largely reclassified within other entities) is idiopathic with no confirmed infectious, environmental, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Acute promyelocytic leukemia with PML-RARA fusion
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Most cases are sporadic with no established cause; the PML::RARA fusion is a somatic event. A recognized subset is therapy-related, caused by prior treatment with topoisomerase II inhibitors (anthracyclines, mitoxantrone, etoposide) or alkylating agents.
cause_source: Mediterr J Hematol Infect Dis therapy-related APL reviews; WHO Classification 5th ed

name: Mixed phenotype acute leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Most cases are sporadic with no established cause; some are therapy-related after prior cytotoxic chemotherapy or radiation. It is defined by acquired genetic lesions and lineage-ambiguous immunophenotype.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: T-lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; it is driven by acquired mutations (e.g., NOTCH1) and is a sporadic malignancy. Prior chemotherapy or radiation and inherited cancer predisposition syndromes contribute to a minority, but most cases are idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Early T-cell precursor lymphoblastic leukemia/lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this immature T-lineage subtype is defined by a distinctive immunophenotype and acquired mutations (FLT3, RAS pathway, DNMT3A). No confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Mast cell leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; like other mastocytoses it is driven by an acquired activating KIT mutation (usually D816V), a somatic event rather than an external cause. No environmental or infectious cause is proven.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Bone marrow mastocytosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is a clonal mast cell disorder driven by an acquired KIT D816V mutation. This somatic driver is not an external cause, and no environmental etiology is confirmed.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; Nature Mod Pathol 2015

name: Indolent systemic mastocytosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; about 95 percent are driven by an acquired KIT D816V gain-of-function mutation, a somatic event, not an external exposure. Rare familial forms exist but the vast majority are sporadic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; Nature Mod Pathol 2015

name: Smoldering systemic mastocytosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is a clonal mast cell disorder driven by an acquired KIT D816V mutation, a somatic driver rather than an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Aggressive systemic mastocytosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is driven by an acquired KIT D816V mutation, usually with additional myeloid mutations (SRSF2, ASXL1, RUNX1). These are somatic events, not external causes.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Systemic mastocytosis with an associated hematologic neoplasm
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; the mast cell clone and the accompanying myeloid neoplasm share acquired mutations (KIT D816V plus SRSF2/ASXL1/RUNX1). These are somatic drivers, not external causes.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Mast cell sarcoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this exceedingly rare localized malignant mast cell tumor is idiopathic, with variable KIT mutation status and no confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Extranodal NK/T-cell lymphoma, nasal type
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by Epstein-Barr virus infection of the neoplastic NK or T cells in genetically susceptible individuals; EBV is present in essentially all cases and is central to pathogenesis. It is most common in East Asian and Latin American populations.
cause_source: Microorganisms 2021 ENKTCL EBV review (PMC8304202); WHO Classification 5th ed

name: Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-positive
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired ALK rearrangement (usually NPM1::ALK), a somatic driver rather than an external cause. No confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Anaplastic large cell lymphoma, ALK-negative
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is a systemic T-cell lymphoma defined by acquired lesions (e.g., DUSP22 or TP63 rearrangements) with no confirmed external cause. It is distinct from the breast implant-associated form.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Breast implant-associated anaplastic large cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by textured-surface breast implants placed for reconstruction or cosmesis; an epidemiologic causal relationship is established and the leading mechanism is chronic inflammation from bacterial biofilm on the textured shell in predisposed individuals. Smooth implants are not implicated.
cause_source: UpToDate BIA-ALCL; FDA 2019 recall of textured implants; Plast Reconstr Surg cohort studies

name: Enteropathy-associated T-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by celiac disease, arising from aberrant intraepithelial T lymphocytes in patients with (often refractory) gluten-sensitive enteropathy. Poor adherence to a gluten-free diet, HLA-DQ2 homozygosity, and late celiac diagnosis are recognized risk factors.
cause_source: Blood 2012;119:2458 (How I treat EATL); WHO Classification 5th ed

name: Monomorphic epitheliotropic intestinal T-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; unlike EATL it is NOT associated with celiac disease. It arises from intestinal intraepithelial T lymphocytes, is more common in Asian and Hispanic populations, and is driven by acquired mutations (e.g., SETD2, STAT5B).
cause_source: Leukemia 2025 (MEITL vs EATL pathways); Pathology Outlines MEITL

name: Indolent T-cell lymphoma of the gastrointestinal tract
aliases: indolent clonal T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder of the gastrointestinal tract
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this indolent clonal T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder of the gut is idiopathic, sometimes with chronic mucosal inflammation, and is driven by acquired lesions (e.g., STAT3::JAK2 fusion, JAK/STAT pathway mutations). No confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Nodal T-follicular helper cell lymphoma, angioimmunoblastic type
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it arises from follicular helper T cells and is driven by acquired mutations (TET2, DNMT3A, IDH2, RHOA G17V). Epstein-Barr virus positive bystander B cells are characteristically present but EBV is not the neoplastic driver.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is idiopathic and frequently associated with autoimmunity. A subset arises in patients with germline HAVCR2 (TIM-3) mutations that predispose to an associated hemophagocytic syndrome, but most cases have no external cause.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas; Nat Genet HAVCR2 studies

name: Adult T-cell leukemia/lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by chronic infection with human T-cell lymphotropic virus type 1 (HTLV-1), typically acquired in infancy through breastfeeding; HTLV-1 is regarded as the sole cause, with disease arising decades later in about 5 percent of carriers. It is endemic in Japan, the Caribbean, sub-Saharan Africa, and parts of South America.
cause_source: Front Microbiol 2012 ATL epidemiology; StatPearls Adult T-Cell Leukemia (NBK558968)

name: Primary cutaneous CD30-positive T-cell lymphoproliferative disorder
aliases: primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this spectrum (lymphomatoid papulosis and primary cutaneous anaplastic large cell lymphoma) is idiopathic. It is defined by CD30 expression and acquired lesions, with no confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas

name: Primary cutaneous gamma/delta T-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this aggressive cutaneous lymphoma of gamma/delta T cells is idiopathic with no confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas

name: Primary cutaneous CD8-positive aggressive epidermotropic cytotoxic T-cell lymphoma
aliases: primary cutaneous aggressive epidermotropic CD8-positive cytotoxic T-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this rare aggressive cutaneous cytotoxic T-cell lymphoma is idiopathic with no confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas

name: Primary cutaneous follicle center lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; it is an indolent cutaneous B-cell lymphoma of follicle center cells. Borrelia burgdorferi has been proposed as a cofactor in some European cases but the association is not confirmed, and most cases are idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas; UpToDate

name: Primary cutaneous marginal zone lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; it is an indolent cutaneous B-cell lymphoma. Chronic antigenic stimulation, including Borrelia burgdorferi infection in some European cohorts, has been implicated as a cofactor but is not confirmed, and most cases are idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas; UpToDate

name: Primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, leg type
aliases: primary cutaneous diffuse large B-cell lymphoma leg type
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this aggressive cutaneous B-cell lymphoma of elderly patients is driven by acquired lesions (MYD88 L265P, BCL2/MYC) with no confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO-EORTC classification of cutaneous lymphomas

name: Primary effusion lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV/HHV-8), which is necessary for the disease; it occurs almost exclusively in the setting of severe immunosuppression, most often advanced HIV/AIDS, and HIV-positive cases are frequently co-infected with Epstein-Barr virus.
cause_source: Systematic review PMC8992617 (classic KSHV/HHV-8 PEL); WHO Classification 5th ed

name: Intravascular large B-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this rare large B-cell lymphoma growing within blood vessels is idiopathic, driven by acquired lesions (frequent MYD88 and CD79B mutations). No confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Primary mediastinal large B-cell lymphoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this thymic B-cell lymphoma of young adults is driven by acquired lesions (9p24/JAK2 and PD-L1/PD-L2 amplification, NF-kB pathway alterations). No confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Langerhans cell sarcoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this rare high-grade Langerhans cell neoplasm is driven by MAPK pathway mutations (including BRAF V600E) and may arise de novo or from progression of Langerhans cell histiocytosis. No confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Juvenile xanthogranuloma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this histiocytic proliferation of infants and children is usually benign and idiopathic, with a subset harboring MAPK pathway mutations. An association with neurofibromatosis type 1 and juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia is recognized in a minority.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; histiocytosis reviews

name: ALK-positive histiocytosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not an external exposure; it is defined by acquired ALK rearrangements (usually KIF5B::ALK), a somatic driver. No infectious, environmental, or hereditary cause is established.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; Blood histiocytosis studies

name: Histiocytic sarcoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; it is a rare malignant histiocytic neoplasm often driven by MAPK pathway mutations. A subset is transdifferentiated (clonally related) from a preceding B-cell lymphoma/leukemia or from germ cell tumors, but no external cause is confirmed.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Interdigitating dendritic cell sarcoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; this rare dendritic cell neoplasm is idiopathic. A minority are clonally related to a preceding low-grade B-cell lymphoma (transdifferentiation), but no external cause is confirmed.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Solitary plasmacytoma of bone
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is a localized clonal plasma cell tumor and part of the plasma cell neoplasm spectrum. No confirmed infectious, environmental, or hereditary cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Extraosseous plasmacytoma
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; this localized extramedullary clonal plasma cell tumor, most often in the upper aerodigestive tract, is idiopathic with no confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Plasma cell leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is an aggressive clonal plasma cell malignancy, arising de novo or evolving from multiple myeloma, driven by acquired cytogenetic abnormalities. No confirmed external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Immunoglobulin light chain amyloidosis
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it results from a clonal plasma cell (or, less often, B-cell) proliferation producing misfolded free light chains that deposit as amyloid. The underlying clone is driven by acquired lesions; no external cause is confirmed.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Chronic neutrophilic leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is a rare myeloproliferative neoplasm driven in most cases by acquired activating CSF3R mutations (T618I), a somatic event rather than an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Atypical chronic myeloid leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is generally not established; it is a BCR::ABL1-negative myelodysplastic/myeloproliferative neoplasm driven by acquired mutations (SETBP1, ETNK1, ASXL1). A subset may relate to prior cytotoxic therapy or benzene exposure, but most cases are idiopathic.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and PDGFRA rearrangement
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired PDGFRA rearrangement (usually the FIP1L1::PDGFRA fusion), a somatic driver rather than an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and PDGFRB rearrangement
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired PDGFRB rearrangement, a somatic tyrosine kinase fusion, not an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and FGFR1 rearrangement
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired FGFR1 rearrangement, a somatic tyrosine kinase fusion, not an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and JAK2 rearrangement
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired JAK2 rearrangement (e.g., PCM1::JAK2), a somatic fusion, not an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and FLT3 rearrangement
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired FLT3 rearrangement, a somatic tyrosine kinase fusion, not an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Myeloid/lymphoid neoplasm with eosinophilia and ETV6::ABL1 fusion
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it is defined by an acquired ETV6::ABL1 fusion, a somatic tyrosine kinase fusion, not an external cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed

name: Juvenile myelomonocytic leukemia
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not an external exposure; it is driven by acquired or, in a subset, germline RAS pathway mutations. Children with neurofibromatosis type 1 and with Noonan syndrome (germline PTPN11) are predisposed, but there is no confirmed infectious or environmental cause.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Chronic myeloid leukemia in blast phase
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established; it represents progression of chronic myeloid leukemia, which is defined by the acquired BCR::ABL1 fusion (Philadelphia chromosome). High-dose ionizing radiation is the only proven environmental risk factor for the underlying CML; the fusion itself is a somatic event.
cause_source: WHO Classification of Haematolymphoid Tumours 5th ed; UpToDate

name: Idiopathic multicentric Castleman disease
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Cause is not established (hence idiopathic); it is an HHV-8-negative systemic inflammatory disorder driven by an unexplained cytokine storm, typically interleukin-6. The source of the hypercytokinemia and any triggering factor remain unknown.
cause_source: Blood 2017;129:1646 (iMCD consensus criteria); Blood 2014;123:2924

name: KSHV/HHV8-associated multicentric Castleman disease
source: WHO-HAEM5 2022
causes: Caused by uncontrolled infection with Kaposi sarcoma-associated herpesvirus (KSHV/HHV-8); it is most often diagnosed in people with HIV infection or other immunosuppression, in whom impaired control of HHV-8 drives the lymphoproliferation.
cause_source: Rare Portal HHV-8+MCD; Blood 2017;129:1646
